title: Structural Anomalies of the Gastrointestinal Tract
authors: Katherine T. Flynn-O'Brien & Samuel E. Rice-Townsend

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Structural GI Anomalies?
GI structural anomalies = หนึ่งในสาเหตุ surgical emergency ที่พบบ่อยที่สุดใน NICU. 6 หลักการสำคัญ: (1) Bilious vomiting = midgut volvulus จนกว่าจะพิสูจน์ได้ว่าไม่ใช่ — ต้อง urgent UGI study; (2) Failure to pass meconium >24h = distal obstruction workup (HD, meconium ileus, ARM, meconium plug); (3) Air in abdomen + EA = distal TEF (Type C, 86%); (4) Double bubble = duodenal atresia → check trisomy 21; (5) Pyloric stenosis = nonbilious vomiting + hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis; (6) VACTERL: EA/TEF + ARM พบ associated anomalies >50% → ต้อง screen ทุกราย.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
รู้ Vogt/Gross classification ของ esophageal atresia (Types A–E) และความถี่แต่ละ type
วินิจฉัย EA/TEF จากอาการ + NG tube coiling + CXR; รู้ว่า air ใน abdomen หมายถึงอะไร
อธิบาย management principles ของ EA/TEF: positioning, NE tube drainage, echocardiogram ก่อน surgery
รู้ long-term complications ของ EA/TEF: anastomotic leak/stricture, GER, tracheomalacia
รู้ว่า bilious vomiting ต้องการ urgent UGI study; อธิบาย Ladd procedure ใน malrotation
แยก duodenal atresia (double bubble) ออกจาก malrotation/volvulus บน X-ray
อธิบาย pyloric stenosis: timing, metabolic derangement, ultrasound criteria, pyloromyotomy
รู้ meconium ileus: CF ใน 90%, soap bubble appearance, water-soluble contrast enema เป็น first-line
วินิจฉัยและ manage Hirschsprung disease: suction rectal biopsy, pull-through surgery
รู้ anorectal malformations: VACTERL workup, colostomy ใน 48h ถ้าไม่มี fistula opening
เข้าใจ Spitz risk classification ของ EA/TEF: ปัจจัยเสี่ยง mortality = prematurity + major CHD
รู้ ankyloglossia: เกือบทุกรายไม่มีปัญหา breastfeeding; frenotomy เมื่อ feeding difficulties persist
1. Disorders of the Oral Cavity and Neck
1.1 Oral Inclusion Cysts — Benign and Self-Limiting
3 types: Epstein pearls (midline palate, keratin cysts), Bohn nodules (alveolar ridges, salivary gland remnants), Dental lamina cysts (midline palate หรือ alveolar ridges)
All benign, asymptomatic; spontaneously rupture and fuse within 5 months; conservative management เหมาะสม — อย่า excise
แยกจาก congenital granular cell epulis (CGCE): smooth red/pink mass จาก maxillary ridge; benign, does NOT grow after birth; excellent prognosis
Oral cavity teratoma (epignathus): arises from upper jaw/palate; may cause airway obstruction if large; almost all require surgical resection
1.2 Tongue Disorders

Ankyloglossia (tongue-tie): most common form = ankyloglossia inferior (short/tight lingual frenulum); most newborns have no breastfeeding difficulty; ถ้า feeding difficulty → lactation evaluation ก่อน; frenotomy ถ้า persist
Ankyloglossia superior (fibrous/osseous tongue-palate connection) — rare แต่ serious: cleft palate, micrognathia, GI malformations, airway obstruction possible
Macroglossia: 4.63/100,000 live births; half isolated, half syndromic; Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome = most common genetic cause; surgery ถ้า airway compromise / dysphagia / dysarthria
1.3 Salivary Gland Disorders
Ranula: mucus cyst from sublingual gland obstruction; simple (intraoral) vs plunging (extends through floor of mouth); observe up to 6 months; excision or sclerotherapy if persists

Neonatal suppurative sialadenitis/parotitis: rare; Staph aureus most common; treat with anti-staph β-lactam + aminoglycoside/3rd-gen cephalosporin; risk: prematurity, prolonged NGT, mechanical ventilation, dehydration, maternal mastitis
Parotid hemangioma: most common salivary gland tumor; rapid growth in first months → spontaneous involution; treatment: propranolol (systemic), intralesional steroids, or surgical resection
1.4 Neck Masses — Branchial Cleft + Thyroid Anomalies

Branchial cleft anomalies: incomplete obliteration of embryonic clefts → cyst/sinus/fistula; 95% from 2nd branchial cleft; lateral neck mass at anterior border SCM; may be asymptomatic or infected; treatment: surgical excision (complete — ไม่งั้น recur); minimal morbidity

Thyroglossal duct cyst: midline neck mass between hyoid and thyroid; propensity to infect; Sistrunk procedure: en bloc excision + middle hyoid bone — reduces recurrence; simple excision → high recurrence
Lingual thyroid: failure of thyroid migration; posterior midline tongue mass; most patients have NO other thyroid tissue; large → airway obstruction

Cystic hygroma: macrocystic lymphatic malformation; posterior triangle neck; may enlarge suddenly with hemorrhage/infection; treatment: surgical excision or sclerotherapy — no clear superiority (Adams et al., Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2012)
2. Esophageal Atresia (EA) and Tracheoesophageal Fistula (TEF)
2.1 Epidemiology and Associated Anomalies
Incidence: 1 in 2500–4500 live births; usually sporadic; recurrence risk sibling < 1%
VACTERL association in ~59% have associated cardiac defects (ASD 46.6%, VSD 21.2%); vertebral/rib defects 25.4%; renal anomalies 21.8%; ARM 11.6%; limb 6.4%
Genetic associations: Down syndrome, Edwards syndrome, CHARGE syndrome, Feingold syndrome, Fanconi anemia
Duodenal atresia co-occurs in 4.7% — even though not part of classic VACTERL
2.2 Classification — Vogt/Gross Types

rows: Pure EA — no fistula; no air in abdomen
EA + proximal fistula (upper pouch TEF only)
EA + DISTAL TEF — air in GI tract; most common type
Isolated TEF without EA; tube passes normally into stomach

blocks.text:
Key Radiograph Interpretation — EA/TEF
NG tube coils at 2nd–4th vertebral body = EA confirmed. Air in abdomen = distal TEF present (Type C). No air in abdomen = pure EA (Type A) หรือ isolated proximal TEF. H-type TEF: tube passes to stomach normally — วินิจฉัยยากเพราะ esophagus intact; ต้องใช้ bronchoscopy หรือ pull-back esophagram.
2.3 Diagnosis
Prenatal: polyhydramnios + small stomach ± dilated cervical esophagus on US; fetal MRI ถ้า US สงสัย
Postnatal: excessive drooling, choking, coughing with feeds → pass NG tube; tube coils at mid-upper thorax (T2–T4) = diagnostic
Confirm distal TEF: air in stomach/intestines on AXR; formal contrast study rarely needed (aspiration risk)
H-type TEF (isolated): bronchoscopy (gold standard) หรือ pull-back esophagram (contrast injected as NG withdrawn from stomach)
2.4 Management
Initial stabilization: head elevated, naso-esophageal (NE) tube drainage of proximal pouch; avoid positive pressure ventilation ถ้าทำได้
Pre-op workup: echocardiogram (CHD in 18–35%; great vessel anomalies affect surgical approach); renal US + spine imaging หลัง surgery
Surgery: divide + close TEF (simple ligation NOT recommended → high recurrence); end-to-end esophageal anastomosis; chest tube to detect anastomotic leak
Staged repair ใน premature/unstable: close TEF + gastrostomy ก่อน; anastomosis delayed until stabilized
Post-op: extubate ASAP; avoid deep suctioning; contrast esophagram day 7 → oral feeds if no leak
2.5 Outcomes and Complications
Overall survival: 93%; Spitz classification: prematurity + major cardiac disease = highest mortality risk (Spitz, J Pediatr Surg 1994;29:723)
Anastomotic leak: up to 20%; most small/contained → heal without reoperation; detect: saliva in chest drain or routine contrast study
Anastomotic stricture: up to 1/3 of cases; ↑ risk ถ้า anastomosis under tension / post-leak; treat with serial dilations
GER: common due to inherent distal esophageal dysmotility; routine acid blockade recommended; many need fundoplication
Tracheomalacia: focal/generalized tracheal weakness; expiratory stridor, barking cough, ALTE; diagnosis by bronchoscopy; severe: aortopexy (suspend aorta to sternum) หรือ posterior tracheopexy
2.6 Laryngotracheoesophageal Cleft (LC)
Rare congenital connection between posterior larynx/trachea และ anterior esophagus; extends distally ตาม 4 types
12% ของ LC patients มี distal TEF; 20% ของ EA/TEF patients มี LC
Diagnosis: rigid bronchoscopy; ETT อาจ fall into esophagus → intubate ให้ distal ต่อ cleft
Minor defects: thickened feeds alone; long defects: major surgery, may need cardiopulmonary bypass
3. Disorders of the Stomach
3.1 Pyloric Stenosis (Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis, HPS)
Incidence: 2–5 per 1000 live births; occurs almost exclusively weeks 3–8 of life; males:females = 4–5:1; more common in white > Black/Asian; identical twin: 25% concordance
Presentation: progressive nonbilious vomiting + dehydration; pyloric "olive" palpable in classic cases

Metabolic derangement: hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis; mechanism: loss of HCl (gastric contents) → contraction alkalosis → RAA activation → reabsorb Na+ in exchange for K+ → hypokalemia
Diagnosis: abdominal ultrasound; criteria: pyloric muscle thickness > 3 mm + length > 15 mm; UGI contrast study if US uncertain
Treatment: correct IV volume FIRST → correct alkalosis (alkalosis → post-GA apnea risk); pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt): incise pyloric muscle → expose submucosa; open or laparoscopic; excellent outcomes
Complications (rare): mucosal perforation, incomplete myotomy; long-term: no functional abnormalities of stomach
3.2 Pyloric Atresia
Rare: 1 in 100,000; 3 types: (1) membrane 57%, (2) solid tissue 34%, (3) complete separation 9%
AXR: dilated stomach, no air distal to stomach; NO dilated proximal duodenum (แยกจาก duodenal atresia)
Screen for epidermolysis bullosa (EB) — strong association; EB + pyloric atresia = poor prognosis but survivors reported
3.3 Gastric Perforation
LBW infants at higher risk (iatrogenic or spontaneous); spontaneous ≠ NEC histology
Presentation: large free air on AXR; peritonitis, sepsis; emergent surgical repair
4. Intestinal Malrotation and Midgut Volvulus — Surgical Emergency
4.1 Normal Rotation and Definition of Malrotation
Normal: midgut returns to abdomen wk 10–12; ligament of Treitz = transition duodenum → jejunum; must be to the LEFT of spine and ABOVE the level of the duodenal bulb
Classic malrotation: duodenum does NOT cross midline → narrow mesenteric vascular pedicle → prone to twist = midgut volvulus
Ladd bands: abnormal retroperitoneal attachments crossing duodenum → obstruction even without volvulus
Associated conditions: gastroschisis, omphalocele, CDH, intestinal atresia, heterotaxy, cardiac defects
4.2 Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis
Volvulus: bilious vomiting (obstruction distal to ampulla of Vater); early = no distention or tenderness; late = ischemia → necrosis → peritonitis → sepsis → death
AXR: gasless abdomen หรือ dilated loops — normal AXR does NOT rule out volvulus

⚠️ EMERGENCY — Bilious Vomiting = Malrotation/Volvulus Until Proven Otherwise
Bilious vomiting ในทารกแรกเกิด = surgical emergency. ต้อง urgent upper GI contrast study เพื่อดูตำแหน่ง ligament of Treitz. อย่ารอสัญญาณของ intestinal ischemia เพราะ midgut volvulus สามารถ infarct entire midgut ได้ภายในชั่วโมง → short bowel syndrome หรือ death.
4.3 Management — Ladd Procedure
Midgut volvulus = open laparotomy; laparoscopy เหมาะแค่ asymptomatic malrotation
Ladd procedure: (1) untwist volvulized bowel, (2) divide Ladd bands, (3) broaden mesentery, (4) rearrange bowel: proximal small bowel to RIGHT, distal ileum/cecum to LEFT upper quadrant
Appendectomy ทำด้วย (appendix relocated → difficult to diagnose future appendicitis in normal position)
Bowel necrosis: resect nonviable bowel; questionable viability → 2nd look surgery after 24–48h
APSA recommendation: asymptomatic malrotation + CHD → observation หรือ delay Ladd ไปจนหลัง cardiac palliation
5. Intestinal Atresias
5.1 Duodenal Atresia
Prevalence: ~1 in 5000 births; etiology: failure of recanalization of lumen
Variants: membranous web, simple occlusion, complete separation; annular pancreas: abnormal fusion of ventral + dorsal pancreas → encircles D2
Associated anomalies: trisomy 21 (Down syndrome), VACTERL, cardiac anomalies, malrotation, ARM, biliary tract anomalies
Prenatal: double bubble on US; polyhydramnios
Postnatal AXR: double bubble sign + NO air distal = duodenal atresia confirmed; double bubble + air distal = stenosis/partial obstruction → UGI ± rule out malrotation

Treatment: gastric decompression + fluid resuscitation → duodenoduodenostomy; laparoscopic or open; excellent outcomes; NOT necessary to rule out distal atresia at time of laparoscopic repair (low incidence)
5.2 Jejunoileal and Colonic Atresia — Classification

rows: Two blind ends connected by cord; repair easier
Distal bowel spirals around marginal vessel; short bowel risk high
Associated with CF; poor prognosis re: bowel length

blocks.text: Etiology: in utero mesenteric vascular compromise (e.g., prenatal volvulus); prevalence ~1.6/10,000 live births; ~1/3 premature
Presentation: bilious vomiting + abdominal distention + failure/delayed passage of meconium; more proximal = earlier symptoms
Workup: AXR (dilated loops, air-fluid levels, no rectal gas) → contrast enema (differentiate from HD, meconium ileus, small left colon syndrome) ± UGI to confirm rotation

Surgery: repair atresia; proximal limb often dilated → may taper rather than resect to preserve length; colonic atresia: diagnose within 72h → reduced morbidity/mortality; colonic atresia associated with HD — consider rectal biopsy
6. Meconium Ileus and Meconium Plug
6.1 Meconium Ileus — Cystic Fibrosis
Meconium ileus: intraluminal obstruction of terminal ileum by abnormal meconium; 90% of cases = cystic fibrosis (CF); up to 20% of CF patients present as newborns with meconium ileus
CF = mutations in CFTR gene → altered bicarbonate + chloride transport → thickened meconium adhering to bowel wall

Uncomplicated meconium ileus: pure obstruction. Complicated: obstruction → twisting → ischemia → ileal atresia and/or intestinal perforation (= "meconium peritonitis": any in utero perforation with meconium spillage)
Prenatal: family history CF / carrier parents; US: hyperechoic bowel, dilation, no gallbladder
AXR: dilated loops + "soap bubble" appearance (air mixed with meconium) — instead of air-fluid levels; calcifications ถ้า prior perforation
Treatment — uncomplicated: water-soluble contrast enema: softens meconium → passes per rectum; repeat if needed; N-acetylcysteine ± hyperosmolar solutions (risk intravascular fluid shifts)
Treatment — failed nonoperative or complicated: laparotomy → irrigation ± ostomy; all meconium ileus patients must undergo CF workup
6.2 Meconium Plug Syndrome
Intraluminal colonic obstruction by abnormal meconium (≠ meconium ileus which is in ileum)
Presentation: delayed meconium, abdominal distention, ± vomiting; contrast enema: diagnostic + therapeutic (usually resolves obstruction)
Must further evaluate for CF AND Hirschsprung disease in all neonates with meconium plug
7. Hirschsprung Disease (HD)
7.1 Pathophysiology and Epidemiology
HD = absence of intrinsic parasympathetic ganglion cells in submucosal + myenteric plexuses; caused by premature arrest of craniocaudal neural crest cell migration
Extent: rectum + sigmoid colon (most common); total colon aganglionosis (TCA) in 10%, ± small bowel involvement
Prevalence: 1 per 5000 live births; male:female = 4:1; highest incidence in Asians (2.8/10,000) and African Americans (2.1/10,000)
Genetics: ~20% have chromosomal abnormalities; many genes implicated but most patients have no defined genetic abnormality
Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis: marked abdominal distention + pain + systemic sepsis; can occur BEFORE or AFTER surgical correction
7.2 Diagnosis
Suspect: failure to pass meconium >24h after birth; feeding intolerance, vomiting, abdominal distention
AXR: dilated loops, paucity of rectal gas
Contrast enema (barium): characteristic rectosigmoid ratio < 1: abnormally small rectum relative to larger sigmoid colon (Fig. 61.15)
Gold standard: suction rectal biopsy → hypertrophic nerve trunks + absence of ganglion cells; can be done at bedside without sedation
7.3 Treatment
Pull-through surgery: excise aganglionic distal colon → "pull-through" of normally innervated proximal bowel to preserved distal rectum above anal canal
Approaches: laparoscopic, open, purely transanal, or hybrid; single-stage or staged (initial ostomy → delayed pull-through)
HD is NOT cured by surgery — anal sphincter remains abnormally innervated; "palliation" not cure; most patients achieve social continence; long-term follow-up essential
8. Anorectal Malformations (ARMs)
8.1 Overview and VACTERL Association
Prevalence: ~1 in 5000 live births; spectrum: anus absent or abnormally located outside sphincter complex
"High" vs "low" based on rectum level relative to levator muscle complex; describe anatomic abnormalities precisely — more predictive of outcomes

VACTERL association in large multicenter review (n=4962): vertebral anomalies 31%, CHD 40%, EA/TEF 7%, genitourinary anomalies 34%, limb defects 7%; 36% met 3+ anomaly VACTERL criteria (Lautz et al., J Pediatr Surg 2015;50:1245)
8.2 Diagnosis and Initial Management
Diagnosis: physical examination; no imaging needed for initial diagnosis; normal anus = halfway between coccyx and scrotum/vaginal orifice
If no anal opening: bowel obstruction → gastric decompression + IV fluids + VACTERL workup
VACTERL workup: echo, spine X-ray, renal US, spinal US
No fistula/anal opening → colostomy within 48h of life; definitive repair planned several months later
8.3 Surgical Repair and Outcomes
Goal: separate rectum from adjacent structures → place into middle of sphincter complex
Posterior sagittal anorectoplasty (PSARP): De Vries & Pena, 1982; most common reconstructive procedure
Similar to HD: surgery is palliative not curative; functional outcomes take years; most can achieve social continence with bowel management program
Prognosis: perineal fistula + normal sacrum → good continence; rectourethral fistula + sacral agenesis → worse continence
9. NICU Practical — Approaching the Surgical Abdomen
9.1 Bilious Vomiting Protocol
Step 1: หยุด feeds, NGT decompression, IV fluids
Step 2: Urgent surgical consult + urgent UGI contrast study → ตรวจตำแหน่ง ligament of Treitz
Step 3: ถ้า malrotation confirmed → emergency laparotomy; Ladd procedure
Step 4: ถ้า UGI ปกติ → rule out duodenal atresia (double bubble), intestinal atresia, HD, meconium ileus
9.2 Failure to Pass Meconium in 24h
ถ้า term baby ไม่ pass meconium ใน 24h → distal obstruction workup
Differential: HD, meconium plug, meconium ileus, ARM, colonic atresia
AXR + contrast enema เป็น first steps; suction rectal biopsy ถ้าสงสัย HD
Meconium plug → contrast enema usually resolves; but workup for CF AND HD ต้องทำ
9.3 Double Bubble on AXR
Double bubble + no distal air = duodenal atresia → urgent surgical consult
Double bubble + air distal = stenosis not complete atresia → still need UGI to rule out malrotation
Screen for Down syndrome (trisomy 21) in ALL duodenal atresia cases; VACTERL workup including echo
10. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Bilious Vomiting = Volvulus Until Proven Otherwise
ไม่ว่า baby จะ look "well" แค่ไหน — bilious vomiting ใน neonate = surgical emergency. Malrotation with midgut volvulus can infarct the entire midgut ภายในชั่วโมง → short bowel syndrome หรือ death. ต้อง urgent UGI series — ไม่ใช่ clinical observation. Normal AXR does NOT rule out volvulus.

PEARL 2 — Air in Abdomen Tells You the EA Type
EA + NG coils at T2–T4: air in stomach/intestines → distal TEF (Type C, 86%). No air → pure EA (Type A). H-type TEF: tube passes normally to stomach; วินิจฉัยยากสุด เพราะ esophagus intact; ต้องใช้ pull-back esophagram หรือ bronchoscopy.

PEARL 3 — Pyloric Stenosis: Fix Electrolytes Before Operating
Pyloric stenosis = nonbilious vomiting + hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. ต้อง correct volume + alkalosis ก่อน surgery เสมอ — alkalosis → post-GA apnea. US criteria: muscle thickness > 3 mm + length > 15 mm. Pyloromyotomy = curative; long-term stomach function ปกติ.

PEARL 4 — Meconium Ileus = CF Until Proven Otherwise
90% of meconium ileus cases = cystic fibrosis. Soap bubble appearance on AXR ≠ air-fluid levels (unlike other atresias). Water-soluble contrast enema = first-line treatment (not surgery); can be repeated. Every meconium ileus patient must undergo CF workup — sweat chloride + CFTR gene panel.

PEARL 5 — HD: Suction Biopsy at Bedside, Pull-Through = Palliation
HD วินิจฉัยด้วย suction rectal biopsy — ทำได้ที่ bedside โดยไม่ต้องให้ sedation. Contrast enema shows rectosigmoid ratio < 1 (small rectum). Pull-through surgery is palliative not curative — anal sphincter ยังคง abnormal innervation; ส่วนใหญ่ achieve social continence แต่ต้อง long-term follow-up.
11. Evidence Update — Landmark Studies
Spitz L, Kiely EM. J Pediatr Surg 1994;29:723–725: Spitz risk classification for EA/TEF; prematurity + major CHD = highest mortality risk; overall survival 93%
Lal DR et al. J Pediatr Surg 2017;52:1245–1251: multicenter perioperative outcomes EA/TEF; anastomotic leak in up to 20%; stricture ~1/3; routine contrast study day 7 standard practice
Lautz TB et al. J Pediatr Surg 2015;50:1245–1250: VACTERL in 4962 EA/TEF + ARM patients; CHD 40% in ARM; 36% met 3+ anomaly criteria; implications for surgical planning
Baird R et al. J Pediatr Surg 2019;54:675–687: APSA systematic review: management of long-gap EA; evidence-based guidelines for staged repair
Graziano K et al. J Pediatr Surg 2015;50:1783–1790: APSA outcomes committee: asymptomatic malrotation + CHD → observation or Ladd after cardiac palliation reasonable
Pandya S, Heiss K. Surg Clin North Am 2012;92:527–539: evidence-based review pyloric stenosis; US criteria (muscle >3 mm, length >15 mm) standard; atropine less effective than pyloromyotomy
MacMahon B. Epidemiology 2006;17:195–201: pyloric stenosis epidemiology; twin concordance 25%; risk factors: erythromycin, prostaglandins, C-section, bottle-feeding
Sathe M, Houwen R. J Cyst Fibros 2017;16(Suppl 2):S32–S39: meconium ileus in CF; 90% CF association; water-soluble contrast enema review; nonoperative success ~60% uncomplicated
Adams SD, Stanton MP. Early Hum Dev 2014;90:921–925: review malrotation and intestinal atresias; intestinal atresia Types I–IV classification; long-term outcomes depend on remaining bowel length
Amiel J et al. J Med Genet 2008;45:1–14: Hirschsprung disease genetics: multiple genes (RET most important); 20% chromosomal abnormalities; male 4:1 female
Wood RJ, Levitt MA. Clin Colon Rectal Surg 2018;31:61–70: anorectal malformations review; PSARP (De Vries & Pena 1982) remains gold standard; prognosis by anatomy + sacral development

Walsh J, Tunkel D. JAMA Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2017;143:1032–1039: ankyloglossia in newborns — systematic review; most require only lactation support; frenotomy reserved for persistent feeding difficulty
Simon LM, Magit AE. Arch Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2012;138:20–24: Sistrunk procedure vs simple excision thyroglossal duct cyst — prior infection increases recurrence even with Sistrunk
Adams MT et al. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2012;147:627–639: systematic review head/neck lymphatic malformation treatment — no conclusive evidence for excision vs sclerotherapy superiority
12. Self-Check Q&A
Q: Esophageal atresia type ไหนพบบ่อยที่สุด และ NG tube coiling + air ใน abdomen แปลว่าอะไร?

ตอบ: Type C (Gross IIIb / Vogt IIIb) พบบ่อยที่สุด — 86%; proximal blind pouch + distal TEF. NG tube coils at T2–T4 = EA confirmed. Air in abdomen = distal TEF present เพราะ air จาก trachea ผ่าน distal fistula → GI tract. No air = pure EA (Type A) หรือ proximal TEF only.
Q: Spitz risk factors สำหรับ mortality ใน EA/TEF คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Spitz J Pediatr Surg 1994: two major risk factors = prematurity (low birth weight) + major congenital heart disease. Overall survival 93%. Group I (>1500 g, no major CHD): 97% survival; Group III (<1500 g + major CHD): ~22% survival. ดังนั้น echocardiogram ก่อน surgery = mandatory — CHD affects surgical approach และ prognosis.
Q: Bilious vomiting ในทารกแรกเกิดต้องทำอะไรก่อน และทำไม?

ตอบ: Urgent upper GI contrast study ทันที — ดูตำแหน่ง ligament of Treitz. ต้อง left of spine + above level of duodenal bulb. เหตุผล: malrotation + midgut volvulus = surgical emergency; ischemia ของ entire midgut เกิดได้ภายในชั่วโมง → short bowel หรือ death. AXR ปกติ ไม่ได้ rule out volvulus. ถ้า UGI confirm malrotation → emergency laparotomy + Ladd procedure.
Q: Pyloric stenosis มี metabolic derangement อะไร และทำไมต้อง correct ก่อน surgery?

ตอบ: Hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. Mechanism: loss of HCl from vomiting → contraction alkalosis → RAA → reabsorb Na+ / excrete K+ → hypokalemia. Alkalosis → post-general anesthesia apnea risk → MUST correct volume + alkalosis BEFORE pyloromyotomy. US criteria: muscle thickness > 3 mm + length > 15 mm. Pyloromyotomy: incise muscle → expose submucosa; excellent long-term results.
Q: Double bubble sign ใน AXR บ่งบอกอะไร และต้องทำอะไรเพิ่ม?

ตอบ: Double bubble (dilated stomach + dilated proximal duodenum) + no distal gas = duodenal atresia. ต้อง: (1) chromosome study / Down syndrome screen, (2) VACTERL workup: echo, renal US, spine imaging, (3) surgical correction: duodenoduodenostomy. Double bubble + air distal → stenosis not complete atresia → UGI ± rule out malrotation.
Q: Meconium ileus วินิจฉัยและ treat อย่างไร? ต้องทำ workup อะไรเพิ่ม?

ตอบ: Presentation: abdominal distention 12–24h, vomiting, no meconium; AXR: dilated loops + soap bubble (not air-fluid levels). First-line: water-soluble contrast enema (therapeutic + diagnostic) — softens meconium → passes per rectum; can repeat. Complicated (ischemia/atresia/perforation) → surgery. All meconium ileus → CF workup mandatory: sweat chloride test + CFTR gene panel (90% CF).
Q: Hirschsprung disease วินิจฉัยอย่างไร และอะไรคือ gold standard?

ตอบ: Clinical suspect: no meconium >24h + abdominal distention + feeding intolerance. Contrast enema: rectosigmoid ratio < 1 (small rectum vs larger sigmoid). Gold standard: suction rectal biopsy — shows hypertrophic nerve trunks + absence of ganglion cells; ทำได้ที่ bedside ไม่ต้อง sedation. Surgery: pull-through procedure = palliative ไม่ใช่ curative; most patients achieve social continence.
Q: VACTERL association ประกอบด้วยอะไร และพบใน structural GI anomalies ไหนบ้าง?

ตอบ: Vertebral, Anorectal, Cardiac, TracheoEsophageal, Renal, Limb. พบใน EA/TEF (CHD 59%, vertebral 25%, renal 22%) และ ARM (CHD 40%, vertebral 31%, GU 34%). ~36% ของ ARM patients meet VACTERL criteria (3+ anomalies). Screen: echo, renal US, spine imaging ทุกราย.
Q: Ladd procedure ประกอบด้วย 4 ขั้นตอนอะไร?

ตอบ: Ladd procedure: (1) untwist volvulized bowel, (2) divide Ladd bands (abnormal retroperitoneal attachments crossing duodenum), (3) broaden the mesentery, (4) rearrange bowel: proximal small bowel RIGHT side, distal ileum/cecum LEFT upper quadrant; appendectomy ด้วย (unpredictable final position). Open laparotomy ถ้า symptomatic; laparoscopy เหมาะแค่ asymptomatic.
Q: Tracheomalacia ใน EA/TEF patients คืออะไร และ treat อย่างไร?

ตอบ: Tracheomalacia: focal/generalized tracheal wall weakness → airway collapse during expiration. Symptoms: expiratory stridor, barking cough, recurrent respiratory illness, ALTE. Diagnosis: bronchoscopy (airway collapse during spontaneous breathing). Severe: aortopexy (suspend aorta + anterior tracheal wall to sternum) หรือ posterior tracheopexy (Shieh et al., J Pediatr Surg 2017). ไม่รุนแรง: CPAP หรือ observation.

Day 62 Preview — Abdominal Wall Defects
พรุ่งนี้เข้าสู่ Avery Ch.62: Abdominal Wall Defects — gastroschisis vs omphalocele: embryology, prenatal diagnosis, delivery planning, surgical closure strategies, silo technique, preformed silos, staged reduction. ต่อเนื่องจากวันนี้ — malrotation พบร่วมเป็น regular feature ใน both conditions.
